data_in.description: Structural cluster DataFrame with columns: Feature, Assay, Isotopologue group, Isotopologue pattern, Adduct group, Adduct, Structural cluster, Correlation cluster, Cross-assay link, and optionally cpdName
Feature metadata table with m/z and retention time values indexed by feature identifier

data_out.description: Interactive HTML network visualization file generated by pyvis.network with nodes colored by correlation cluster, edges weighted by link type, and optional labels
NetworkX graph object with nodes (features) and weighted edges (structural and correlation links) that can be saved, exported, or imported into Cytoscape for manual curation

domain_knowledge: Edge weights in metabolomics structural networks are assigned numerically to represent biological link types: isotopologue patterns (weight 1) indicate same molecular structure at different mass isotopes; adduct variants (weight 5) represent the same neutral mass in different ionization states; cross-assay links (weight 10) connect features across different LC-MS assays using [M+H]+/[M-H]- mass-neutral equivalence; correlation cluster co-membership (weight 15) groups features by statistical covariance.

NetworkX graph serialization for Cytoscape import requires explicit node and edge attribute dictionaries (feature ID, m/z, RT, cluster assignments, compound annotations) to preserve metadata during round-trip curation and reimport.

Node coloring by correlation cluster in pyvis visualization distinguishes statistical groupings (determined by hierarchical clustering on Pearson/Kendall/Spearman correlation matrices) from structural groupings (isotopologue + adduct), enabling visual separation of mechanistic vs. statistical relationships.

The include_all parameter controls graph sparsity: when False, only features with at least one structural or correlation edge are rendered, reducing visual clutter; when True, isolated features appear as singleton nodes, useful for detecting unannotated singletons.

Interactive HTML output from pyvis is browser-based and allows runtime manipulation (panning, zooming, node dragging); NetworkX object export supports offline analysis, batch curation scripts, and integration with external graph tools.

[discussion] Specific documentation or example code for the graph construction step (conversion of structural cluster assignments to NetworkX/pyvis artifacts) is not provided in the discussion section.: 'Source: github:kopeckylukas__py-mamsi'

cmd: import pandas as pd; import networkx as nx; from pyvis.network import Network; import json; structural_clusters = pd.read_csv('{inputs.structural_clusters}'); feature_metadata = pd.read_csv('{inputs.feature_metadata}', index_col=0); G = nx.Graph(); features = structural_clusters['Feature'].unique(); [G.add_node(f, assay=structural_clusters[structural_clusters['Feature']==f]['Assay'].iloc[0] if len(structural_clusters[structural_clusters['Feature']==f]) > 0 else None) for f in features]; edge_configs = [('Isotopologue group', 1), ('Adduct group', 5), ('Cross-assay link', 10), ('Correlation cluster', 15)]; [G.add_edge(row['Feature'], row['Feature'], weight=w, link_type=link_type) for _, row in structural_clusters.iterrows() for link_type, w in edge_configs if pd.notna(row.get(link_type))]; net = Network(directed=False, height='750px', width='100%'); net.from_nx(G); net.show('{task_id}_network.html'); nx.write_gpickle(G, '{task_id}_graph.pkl'); print('Network graph and visualization generated successfully')
executable.grounding_notes: Unverified by the executable guard — command runs inline code (-c/-e); its API calls cannot be validated against package files. Treat this command as a best-effort inference; verify the entry point and APIs against the real code before running.

finding: MAMSI can save structural clusters as a NetworkX object and review them in Cytoscape for interactive network visualization and analysis of structural relationships.
inputs: Structural cluster DataFrame with columns: Feature, Assay, Isotopologue group, Isotopologue pattern, Adduct group, Adduct, Structural cluster, Correlation cluster, Cross-assay link, and optionally cpdName

methodology_summary: Extract structural and correlation cluster assignments from upstream MamsiStructSearch outputs (isotopologue groups, adduct groups, cross-assay links, correlation cluster labels).
Instantiate NetworkX graph and populate nodes with feature identifiers and node attributes (assay, m/z, RT, cluster memberships, optional compound annotations).
Encode structural relationships as weighted edges: isotopologue links (weight=1), adduct links (weight=5), cross-assay links (weight=10), correlation co-membership (weight=15).
Optionally filter to linked features only or include all features based on include_all parameter.
Render interactive pyvis.network visualization with spring layout, node coloring by correlation cluster, and edge thickness scaled by link weight; save to HTML file.

Validation: verify NetworkX object contains correct node count, edge count, and edge weight distribution matching structural cluster topology; confirm HTML renders without JavaScript errors and interactive features (pan, zoom, node drag) are functional.

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: networkx, pyvis, pandas, Cytoscape, Interactive HTML network visualization file generated by pyvis.network with nodes colored by correlation cluster, edges weighted by link type, and optional labels, NetworkX graph object with nodes (features) and weighted edges (structural and correlation links) that can be saved, exported, or imported into Cytoscape for manual curation.
workflow_description: 1. Load structural cluster data (isotopologue groups, adduct groups, and cross-assay links) along with correlation cluster assignments from upstream MamsiStructSearch outputs. 2. Create NetworkX graph with features as nodes, assigning node attributes (assay, m/z, retention time, cluster membership, compound annotation) extracted from feature metadata. 3. Add edges between nodes based on structural link type: assign weight 1 for isotopologue links, weight 5 for adduct links, weight 10 for cross-assay links, and weight 15 for correlation cluster co-membership. 4. Optionally include all features or filter to structurally linked features only via include_all parameter. 5. Generate interactive pyvis.network visualization with spring layout, node coloring by correlation cluster membership, and edge thickness proportional to link weight; save as HTML artifact. 6. Return NetworkX object for downstream curation in Cytoscape or additional programmatic analysis.
